file_name: Gudiu and Stoicu-Tivadar - 2024 - Macro vs micro skin imaging Finding an affordable approach for dermatological care access in rural.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) implemented via MATLAB's Deep Network Designer with Transfer Learning (SqueezeNet architecture) to classify skin lesions as melanoma or non-melanoma. The model was trained on dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive and applied to macro and micro smartphone images to evaluate diagnostic performance.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the acquisition of skin lesion images using smartphone macro and micro lenses, the training of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) using Transfer Learning on the ISIC dataset, and the evaluation of diagnostic performance (75% accuracy for micro images vs 63% for macro images) on a specific dataset of 15 nevus formations. It includes sections for Methods, Results, and Discussion of new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Macro vs Micro Skin Imaging: Finding an Affordable Approach for Dermatological Care Access in Rural/Remote Areas
journal: Collaboration across Disciplines for the Health of People, Animals and Ecosystems
brief_summary: This study evaluated the feasibility of using smartphone clip-on macro and micro lenses as an affordable alternative to dermatoscopes for remote melanoma diagnosis in rural areas. A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on the SqueezeNet architecture was trained via transfer learning on dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive to classify skin lesions. The model was subsequently tested on images of 15 nevus formations captured by the smartphone lenses. The CNN achieved a 75% correct labeling accuracy on microscopic images compared to 63% on macroscopic images. The results indicate that smartphone-based micro-imaging offers superior diagnostic detail and accuracy for teledermatology in resource-limited settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as a target class for binary classification (melanoma vs non-melanoma) in the CNN training on the ISIC dataset (314 melanoma lesions) and as a predicted label for the test images.
Used as the comparator class for binary classification (melanoma vs non-melanoma) in the CNN training on the ISIC dataset (314 non-melanoma lesions) and as a predicted label for the test images.

The specific clinical entity of the 15 images acquired from one subject for the macro/micro imaging experiment. These were confirmed to be nevi by a dermatologist and subsequently labeled by the CNN as either melanoma or non-melanoma.

all_task_types.details: The study employed a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on the SqueezeNet architecture to classify skin lesions into two discrete categories: melanoma or non-melanoma. The model was trained on dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive and subsequently applied to macro and micro smartphone images of nevus formations to predict their diagnostic category. Performance was measured by the percentage of correctly labeled images (75% for micro, 63% for macro).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (Training/Validation/Testing Split)
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) open source archive, used for training the CNN via transfer learning. The dataset consisted of melanoma and non-melanoma lesions which were rotated at 90°, 180°, and 270° to augment the data.

use.training: Used to train the SqueezeNet CNN via transfer learning (70% of augmented data).
use.validation: Used for validation during training (20% of augmented data).
use.testing: Used for testing during training (10% of augmented data).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (Additional Test Set)
datasets_used.description: A separate dermoscopic dataset from the ISIC database, not included in the training data, used to perform an additional test session on the trained network.

use.testing: Used for an additional test session to evaluate network performance on unseen dermoscopic data (achieved 78% accuracy).

datasets_used.name: Subject Nevus Formations (Macro/Micro Smartphone Images)
datasets_used.description: Images of 15 nevus formations from one individual, captured using smartphone clip-on macro and microscopic lenses. The images were rotated to create multiple perspectives. These images were labeled by a CNN as melanoma or non-melanoma.

use.testing: Used as the primary test set to evaluate the diagnostic performance of the CNN on macro vs. micro smartphone imaging (75% accuracy for micro, 63% for macro).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared diagnostic accuracy (75% for micro images vs. 63% for macro images) using descriptive statistics and confidence percentages without statistical inference.

ISIC_Archive_Training_Set.notes: The network was trained on 2512 dermoscopic images (314 melanoma/314 non-melanoma lesions, rotated at 90°, 180°, and 270°). The 78% accuracy was reported on an additional test session using a dermoscopic dataset from the ISIC database (50 melanoma/50 non-melanoma formations) not included in the training data.

Subject_Nevus_Formations_Macro_Images.notes: 60 images (15 nevus formations rotated at 90°, 180°, and 270°) captured using a smartphone macro lens. The CNN labeled 63% of them correctly. More than half of the mislabeled images had a confidence in label of over 90%.

Subject_Nevus_Formations_Micro_Images.notes: 60 images (15 nevus formations rotated at 90°, 180°, and 270°) captured using a smartphone microscopic lens. The CNN labeled 75% of them correctly. The microscopic lens provided better view, revealing more details about color distribution and border compared to macro pictures.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained using dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) open source archive via Transfer Learning.

training_details.dataset: ISIC archive (314 melanoma/314 non-melanoma lesions, rotated at 90°, 180°, and 270°, totaling 2512 images)
training_details.additional_test: 50 melanoma/50 non-melanoma formations from ISIC database not included in training data

preprocessing_techniques: Image rotation at 90°, 180°, and 270° for data augmentation (applied to both ISIC training data and subject nevus images)
Use of transparent gel to reduce light reflection and capture color details (applied to macro lens images)
Use of camera zoom functionality (applied to macro lens images to compensate for lack of incorporated light)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated at 90°, 180°, and 270° to maximize the number of input data and offer different perspectives. Applied to both the ISIC training dataset (314 melanoma/314 non-melanoma lesions) and the subject nevus formations (15 formations).

conclusions: Smartphone-based imaging with specialized micro lenses has the potential to improve diagnostic accuracy for melanoma and other dermatological conditions in remote healthcare settings.
The Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) demonstrated better performance on microscopic images (75% correct labeling) compared to macroscopic images (63% correct labeling).
Microscopic lens images provided better visual detail regarding color distribution and borders compared to macro images.
Macro lens images tended to capture less color variation, presenting formations with a more even appearance than their actual coloring.
More than half of the mislabeled macro images had a high confidence rate (over 90%), emphasizing the importance of detailed pictures for viable remote diagnosis.
The study is limited by the exclusive use of nevus images and the lack of collaboration with a dermatologist for validation and diverse lesion types.
Smartphones equipped with magnifying lenses can serve as an affordable alternative for underserved areas lacking access to advanced diagnostic tools like dermatoscopes.
Involving general practitioners in primary care settings can help uniformize healthcare access, particularly for elderly populations less accustomed to technology.
important_remarks: The study is limited by the exclusive use of nevus formations from a single subject, lacking diverse lesion types (e.g., actual melanomas) for the macro/micro imaging experiment.
There was no collaboration with a dermatologist for the validation of the smartphone-captured images, relying instead on a CNN trained on ISIC data to label the nevi as melanoma or non-melanoma.
The CNN was trained on dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive and applied to smartphone macro/micro images, representing a domain shift that may affect generalizability.
The macro lens setup required additional steps (transparent gel, camera zoom) to mitigate shadowing and light reflection issues, which impacted image stability and quality.
The microscopic lens provided a narrower field of view (200x magnification), potentially limiting the assessment of lesion borders and overall context.

More than half of the mislabeled macro images had a high confidence rate (over 90%), indicating that the model was confidently incorrect, highlighting the risk of high-confidence errors in low-detail imaging.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to compare the 75% accuracy of micro images versus 63% for macro images.
The external validation on the ISIC dermoscopic dataset achieved 78% accuracy, but this does not directly validate the performance on the smartphone-captured clinical images.
